findings.title: projects.findAll default limit is 1000 — effectively unbounded for admins
findings.description: The default limit is 1000 and the hard ceiling is also 1000, so any caller without an explicit limit gets up to 1000 project rows per page. For ADMIN/RESPONSABLE/MANAGER roles (hasFullVisibility = true) this applies to the full projects table with eager-loaded members (take: 5 each), createdBy, manager, sponsor, clients, and all _count sub-selects. With 500 projects this generates a JOIN tree with 500×5 = 2500 ProjectMember rows hydrated per request.
findings.root_cause: Default and ceiling limit values are both 1000; there is no differentiation between list-page use (needs ~20) and export use (needs all).
findings.impact: A single request from an ADMIN role fetches up to 1000 projects with 4 JOINed relations each. At 500 projects, response payloads can exceed 1 MB and Prisma will hydrate thousands of related rows. Response times will scale super-linearly as the database grows.
findings.suggested_fix: Lower the default limit to 20 and the hard ceiling to 200 for the standard list endpoint; provide a separate, authenticated export endpoint without a page limit. Or at minimum align with tasks.findAll's default of 10.
acceptance_criteria: GET /projects without explicit limit parameter returns at most 20 items
GET /projects?limit=201 is rejected with 400 or silently capped at 200
An export endpoint (CSV or JSON) exists for ADMIN with explicit ceiling
findings.notes: tasks.findAll uses default 10 but ceiling 1000 — the ceiling issue is shared.

findings.title: tasks.findAll hard ceiling is 1000 — same page can carry a very large payload
findings.description: The default is 10 (good) but any caller can pass limit=1000 and receive up to 1000 tasks in a single page. Each task row includes the full assignee, all TaskAssignee rows (each with user fields), and four _count subqueries. With 1000 tasks and 5 assignees each, this hydrates 5000 TaskAssignee+User rows per request.
findings.root_cause: Ceiling is set to 1000 which matches the default limit of the same function's fall-through (`limit || 1000`), allowing effective bypass of pagination.
findings.impact: A single request with limit=1000 on a large project can transfer several hundred KB of JSON and require Prisma to hydrate thousands of nested rows. At scale this causes OOM on the Node process or multi-second query times.
findings.suggested_fix: Lower the ceiling to 100 for the standard list. If a bulk export is needed add a dedicated endpoint that streams CSV directly from the DB without hydrating JS objects.
acceptance_criteria: GET /tasks?limit=101 is capped at 100
findAll safeLimit formula uses a ceiling of 100 not 1000

findings.title: getTasksByProject is completely unbounded — no skip/take on all project tasks
findings.description: This method returns every task for a project with no pagination (no take/skip). It includes dependencies (with dependsOnTask), assignees with user details, subtasks, and _count subqueries. A project with 500 tasks, each with 3 subtasks and 2 dependencies, produces a response of thousands of rows. This endpoint is called from the project detail page and task management views.
findings.root_cause: No pagination was added to the project-scoped task list; the caller (frontend project detail page) is expected to receive all tasks at once.
findings.impact: A large project with hundreds of tasks will cause multi-second Prisma query times and multi-MB JSON responses. The Node.js serialization of such a large include graph can exhaust heap memory.
findings.suggested_fix: Add skip/take with a reasonable page size (50-100) and return a paginated result, or at minimum add a hard cap of 500 rows via take. The frontend should use virtual scrolling or lazy loading for large task lists.
acceptance_criteria: GET /tasks?projectId=X never returns more than 200 tasks in a single response
getTasksByProject adds a hard take cap or pagination parameters

findings.title: getTasksByAssignee is completely unbounded — loads all tasks for a user with time entries
findings.description: Returns all tasks ever assigned to a user (primary or multi-assignee) without any limit. Includes assignees list, _count subqueries for 4 relations, and all non-dismissal time entries. A user with 200 completed tasks each with 10 time entries generates 2000 TimeEntry rows fetched in memory for the aggregation. The dashboard calls this on every load.
findings.root_cause: No pagination; inline time-entry aggregation loads all rows to compute totalLoggedHours in JS.
findings.impact: On the dashboard page this runs on every page load for every user. A power user with hundreds of tasks causes a full table scan of task_assignees + tasks + time_entries on each visit.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a take limit (e.g. 200) and use a groupBy aggregation on time_entries server-side instead of loading all rows. Or split into active tasks (PENDING/IN_PROGRESS) and a separate history endpoint.
acceptance_criteria: getTasksByAssignee returns at most 200 tasks
totalLoggedHours is computed via a DB aggregate not JS reduce over hydrated rows

findings.title: findOrphans() has no pagination or limit — returns all orphan tasks
findings.description: Returns every orphan task (projectId IS NULL) with no limit. This set grows unboundedly as recurring meetings, cross-cutting tasks, and so on accumulate. The include brings assignees and their user profiles, plus three _count subqueries. There is no pagination parameter exposed.
findings.root_cause: The method was never given a pagination interface; the design assumes orphan tasks are few.
findings.impact: An organisation that creates many meeting/cross-cutting tasks will accumulate hundreds of orphan tasks. The endpoint becomes progressively slower and the payload grows without bound.
findings.suggested_fix: Add pagination (page/limit) with a default of 20 and a ceiling of 200.
acceptance_criteria: GET /tasks/orphans returns paginated results with a meta object
Hard cap of 200 per page enforced

findings.title: getMyDoneUndeclaredTasks() is unbounded — DONE tasks without time entries grow indefinitely
findings.description: Fetches every DONE task assigned to the user that has no user time entry. There is no limit. The correlated NOT EXISTS subquery on time_entries runs on every row in the tasks table that matches the assignee filter. This is called on every dashboard load for each user. Old users with many archived DONE tasks get progressively slower results.
findings.root_cause: No pagination; the anti-join subquery (NOT: timeEntries.some) is correlated and forces a row-by-row evaluation.
findings.impact: Dashboard load time scales with historical task count. With 1000 historical DONE tasks and no time entries declared, this scans the full set every visit.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a hard cap (take: 50) since the widget only displays a few items. Consider adding a dedicated dismissal flag to avoid the expensive NOT EXISTS correlated subquery.
acceptance_criteria: getMyDoneUndeclaredTasks returns at most 50 tasks
Dashboard load is not blocked by historical DONE task accumulation

findings.title: importTasks: sequential await inside for-loop — O(N) DB round-trips for N tasks
findings.description: The import loop fires at least 2 sequential DB round-trips per task (findFirst + create) and an additional round-trip per subtask. For a 100-row CSV with 5 subtasks per task this is 600+ sequential DB calls. These are not batched and cannot be parallelised with Promise.all because each row's create depends on the duplicate check result. The outer task creation loop compounds with the inner subtask loop.
findings.root_cause: Row-by-row processing with individual findFirst + create calls; subtasks are also created one-by-one inside a nested loop.
findings.impact: A 100-task CSV import takes 600+ sequential DB round-trips (est. 600ms–6s on a local network, 6–60s on a remote DB). Import UX will time out for large files.
findings.suggested_fix: 1) Batch-fetch all existing titles in one query before the loop (already done for usersByEmail, apply same pattern for titles). 2) Batch-create tasks using createMany. 3) Batch-create subtasks using a single createMany after all task IDs are collected.
acceptance_criteria: importTasks issues at most O(1) DB queries regardless of CSV row count
A 100-row CSV import completes in under 500ms
findings.notes: validateImport does pre-fetch existing titles correctly (existingTasks set) — the same pattern should be applied to importTasks.

findings.title: importUsers: sequential await per user inside for-loop — O(N) DB round-trips
findings.description: The user import loop makes at least 3 sequential awaits per row: findFirst (duplicate check), bcrypt.hash (CPU-bound, ~100ms at cost 12), and user.create. Plus a conditional userService.createMany. For a 50-user CSV import this means 150+ DB/CPU operations run sequentially. bcrypt.hash at cost 12 takes ~100ms per call on modern hardware, so 50 users = 5 seconds of hashing alone.
findings.root_cause: Each row processed individually; duplicate check and bcrypt are both blocking the loop.
findings.impact: A 50-user CSV import takes ~5–10s just for bcrypt hashing, plus DB round-trips. The HTTP request will time out on large imports.
findings.suggested_fix: Pre-fetch all existing emails/logins in one query (as done in validateImport). Run bcrypt.hash calls in parallel with Promise.all for a batch, then createMany. Cap batch size at 20 to avoid overwhelming the event loop.
acceptance_criteria: A 50-user import completes in under 2s
Duplicate check uses a pre-fetched Set not per-row findFirst
findings.notes: validateImport correctly pre-fetches existingUsers in one query — importUsers should mirror that pattern.

findings.title: checkCircularDependency uses a BFS with one DB query per visited node — O(E) round-trips
findings.description: The cycle-detection BFS issues one taskDependency.findMany per BFS node. For a project with a dependency chain of depth 10 this fires 10 sequential DB round-trips. In the worst case (complete DAG of N nodes), this is O(N) sequential round-trips. Since there is no depth limit, a deep dependency graph causes multi-second latency on addDependency calls.
findings.root_cause: BFS implemented with one async DB call per level/node rather than a recursive CTE or a full graph fetch.
findings.impact: addDependency on a project with a deep dependency chain causes latency proportional to chain depth. At depth 20 this is 20 sequential queries (~200ms on a local DB, potentially 2s on a remote DB).
findings.suggested_fix: Replace with a single Postgres recursive CTE query: `WITH RECURSIVE deps AS (SELECT depends_on_task_id FROM task_dependencies WHERE task_id = $1 UNION ALL ...) SELECT * FROM deps WHERE depends_on_task_id = $2 LIMIT 1`. This runs in one round-trip regardless of graph depth.
acceptance_criteria: checkCircularDependency issues exactly 1 DB query regardless of dependency chain depth
A chain of 50 dependencies is checked in under 10ms

findings.title: analytics.getTasks fetches all task rows with no limit — unbounded full-table scan
findings.description: This method returns the full Task[] (all columns) with no limit for the entire analytics scope. The result is then used in calculateMetrics (filter operations in JS) and getProjectDetails (groupBy by projectId in JS). The duplicate groupBy query in getAnalytics (taskStatusGroupBy, line 114) already computes the counts needed; getTasks exists to feed calculateMetrics but only uses status and endDate fields, fetching all task columns is wasteful.
findings.root_cause: getTasks was written to return full Task objects for all downstream consumers; it was not updated when the groupBy path was added.
findings.impact: Analytics for an admin with 50 projects and 2000 tasks fetches 2000 full Task rows (including description, dates, and so on) into Node memory, then filters them in JS. This is redundant because taskStatusGroupBy already has all needed count data.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace getTasks entirely with the taskStatusGroupBy result. Update calculateMetrics to consume aggregated counts. Remove the full-table task scan from the analytics hot path.
acceptance_criteria: getAnalytics issues zero calls to task.findMany after this fix
calculateMetrics consumes only the groupBy result, not a full Task[]
findings.notes: taskStatusGroupBy on line 114 is redundant with the groupBy inside getProjects on line 189. There are now TWO groupBy queries on task per analytics call.

findings.title: analytics.getAnalytics fires TWO identical task.groupBy queries per request
findings.description: getAnalytics fires taskStatusGroupBy (line 113) over the projectIds from getProjects. But getProjects itself (line 189) also fires an identical task.groupBy by ['projectId','status'] for progress computation. That means every analytics request runs the same GROUP BY on the tasks table twice. Both queries scan the same rows with the same filter.
findings.root_cause: getProjects was refactored to compute progress via groupBy without removing or sharing the outer groupBy that was added later.
findings.impact: Every analytics page load fires one redundant DB query that scans all task rows in scope. On a tenant with 50 projects and 2000 tasks this is a wasted full-table-equivalent group scan.
findings.suggested_fix: Pass the result of the outer taskStatusGroupBy (line 113) into getProjects as a parameter, eliminating the inner groupBy on line 189. Or compute the per-project progress map in getAnalytics and pass it through.
acceptance_criteria: getAnalytics issues exactly ONE task.groupBy per request
Both getProjectProgressData and per-project progress in getProjects use the same pre-computed map

findings.title: analytics members include: loads ALL project members with full user object — no per-project cap
findings.description: getProjects in analytics includes ALL ProjectMember rows for each project, each with a full User object and their Department. Unlike the main project list (which uses take: 5 for members), the analytics query has no limit on members. A project with 50 members multiplied by 50 projects generates 2500 full user rows fetched per analytics call.
findings.root_cause: No take limit on members include in the analytics getProjects path.
findings.impact: Analytics for tenants with many project members causes a combinatorial explosion of hydrated rows. A 50-project tenant with 50 members per project fetches 2500 user+department rows on each analytics page load (60s TTL cache partially mitigates this).
findings.suggested_fix: Add `take: 5` on members include as done in projects.findAll, or better, remove the members include from analytics entirely since the member count is available via _count.members.
acceptance_criteria: analytics members include has a take: 5 cap or is removed
Member user data is not fetched for each of the 50 analytics projects

findings.title: Analytics cache has no mutation invalidation — stale data served for up to 60s after changes
findings.code_evidence:
/** TTL for analytics cache entries (seconds). Balances freshness vs. Prisma load. */
const ANALYTICS_CACHE_TTL = 60;
findings.description: The analytics cache uses TTL-only eviction (60 seconds) with no mutation-bust hooks. When a task is created, completed, or a project status changes, the analytics page continues to show stale data for up to 60 seconds. The CacheService comment acknowledges this: 'TTL-only eviction (no mutation-bust hooks) within this initial bounded scope (AC#6 forbids touching mutation service paths in this commit).' The AC#6 block may still be active.
findings.root_cause: Cache invalidation was deferred to a future commit (AC#6 constraint) and has not been implemented.
findings.impact: Users who change a task status see their analytics unchanged for up to 60 seconds. In a real-time dashboard context this degrades trust in the data. For high-write scenarios (task status transitions during a sprint), the cache hit rate will be high but the data will frequently be stale.
findings.suggested_fix: Add cache invalidation calls in TasksService.update (status change), ProjectsService.update/archive/unarchive, and LeavesService.approve. Use pattern-delete `cache:analytics:${userId}:*` or a dedicated invalidation key per user-scope.
acceptance_criteria: After a task status change, the next analytics request returns fresh data
CacheService.del is called with the analytics key on task/project mutations
findings.notes: The CacheService comment on line 12 explicitly acknowledges this gap.

findings.title: audit_logs advisory lock serialises ALL audit writes globally — creates a bottleneck under concurrent mutations
findings.code_evidence: await tx.$executeRaw`SELECT pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext('audit_logs_chain'))`;
findings.description: Every audit log write acquires the same global advisory lock on the string 'audit_logs_chain'. This serialises all audit inserts across all concurrent API requests. Under moderate load (10 concurrent requests each triggering an audit write), all 10 requests queue on this lock. Since the lock is held for the duration of a transaction that includes a user lookup + INSERT, hold time can be 5-20ms per write, creating throughput bottleneck of 50-200 audit writes/second.
findings.root_cause: The hash-chained audit log requires strictly ordered inserts to maintain prevHash integrity; the advisory lock is the implementation for this ordering guarantee.
findings.impact: Under concurrent load (e.g. bulk import of 50 users via importUsers), each user creation triggers an audit write. All 50 audit writes queue serially on the advisory lock, adding ~1s of advisory lock wait time to the import. More broadly, any API endpoint that triggers audit writes becomes a contention point.
findings.suggested_fix: Consider whether the strict sequential chain is necessary for ALL audit entries, or only for sensitive actions (ROLE_CHANGE, USER_DELETED, and so on). For high-frequency routine events, use a background queue (Redis list + consumer) that batches inserts with a single advisory lock acquisition. Alternatively, use a monotonic sequence column and accept eventual chain consistency.
acceptance_criteria: Bulk import of 50 users completes without measurable advisory lock wait accumulation
Advisory lock contention is documented as a known tradeoff if not fixed
findings.notes: This is an architectural tradeoff — the hash chain's correctness depends on serialisation. The issue is the scale impact, not the design intent.

findings.title: planning.getOverview loads up to 1000 users and 1000 leaves in a single blocking request
findings.description: The planning overview endpoint hard-codes limit=1000 for both users (allowFullScan: true bypasses the 200 hard ceiling) and services. Combined with leavesService.findAll(1, 1000, ...) in the second parallel batch (line 96-105), this single endpoint loads up to 1000 users + 1000 services + up to 500 tasks + up to 1000 leaves in one request. The response JSON for a mid-sized organisation (200 users, 50 services, 300 leaves) will easily exceed 1 MB.
findings.root_cause: The planning view was designed to render a full grid of all users/leaves/tasks for a date range; the 1000 limits were set as 'practical' ceilings but are effectively unbounded for small-to-medium organisations.
findings.impact: Planning page load for a 500-user organisation triggers 3000+ row hydrations in a single request. Response latency and payload size degrade the planning page UX significantly. The Node.js process must serialise several MB of JSON per request.
findings.suggested_fix: Implement pagination or date-windowed loading for the planning view. Load users and services once (cached), then page-load leaves/tasks. Alternatively, move to a per-service or per-department view that scopes the load.
acceptance_criteria: Planning overview does not load more than 200 users in a single request
Leaves and tasks are loaded for a maximum 4-week window by default

findings.title: projects.getProjectStats fetches full members list (no select) — pulls all member columns
findings.description: In getProjectStats, `members: true` includes every column of every ProjectMember row (id, projectId, userId, role, allocation, startDate, endDate, createdAt) for every member. The method only uses `project.members.length` to compute totalMembers. All other member data is fetched but discarded.
findings.root_cause: members: true was written for convenience; only the count is needed.
findings.impact: For a project with 100 members, this fetches 100 full ProjectMember rows when only the count is needed. The fix is trivial but worth flagging as the stats endpoint is called from the project detail page.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `members: true` with `_count: { select: { members: true } }` and update the return path to read `project._count.members`.
acceptance_criteria: getProjectStats no longer includes the full members array
_count.members is used for the totalMembers field

findings.title: getSnapshots has no pagination or limit — returns all historical snapshots for a project
findings.description: Returns all ProjectSnapshot rows for a project with no limit. For a project running for 2 years with daily snapshots, this is 730 rows. Without a date filter (from/to are optional), all snapshots for the project's lifetime are returned in a single query.
findings.root_cause: No pagination or default date window; both from and to are optional with no fallback.
findings.impact: A long-running project's snapshot history grows at 1 row/day indefinitely. Fetching all snapshots for a chart without a date filter returns the full history on every request.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a default date window (e.g. last 90 days) when from/to are not provided, and add a hard limit of 365 rows.
acceptance_criteria: getSnapshots with no date filter returns at most the last 90 days
A hard take limit of 365 is enforced

findings.title: getProjectStats issues 2 separate TimeEntry findMany — should use one groupBy or aggregate
findings.description: getProjectStats fetches all user time entries and all third-party time entries for a project's task IDs, then reduces them in JavaScript. For a project with 100 tasks and 500 time entries, this fetches 500 rows just for the hours column. The aggregation should be pushed to Postgres via aggregate()._sum.hours.
findings.root_cause: findMany used where aggregate is appropriate; JS reduce replaces a DB SUM.
findings.impact: A project with 1000 time entries fetches 1000 rows to compute two SUM values. Using aggregate() would return the same result in a single row.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace both findMany calls with `this.prisma.timeEntry.aggregate({ _sum: { hours: true }, where: {...} })` for each dimension.
acceptance_criteria: getProjectStats uses timeEntry.aggregate not findMany for hour totals
No JS reduce over time entry arrays in getProjectStats

findings.title: captureSnapshots loads all ACTIVE project tasks in memory — no streaming or groupBy
findings.description: captureSnapshots loads all ACTIVE projects with their full task status arrays and milestone arrays into Node memory. For 100 active projects each with 200 tasks, this is 20,000 task status rows loaded into memory. The status counts are then computed with JS .filter().length — three passes per project. This runs on a scheduled timer tick.
findings.root_cause: The comment mentions 'PER-003: batched — 2 DB queries' but the first query still loads all task rows (select: { status: true }) into Node memory for JS aggregation instead of using a groupBy.
findings.impact: With 100 active projects and 200 tasks each, the snapshot scheduler loads 20,000 task rows every time it runs. A Prisma groupBy would return exactly N(projects)×N(statuses) = ~500 rows.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the nested tasks select with a groupBy on task by ['projectId', 'status'] over all active project IDs. Same for milestones if volume warrants it.
acceptance_criteria: captureSnapshots uses task.groupBy not task select inside project include
Node memory footprint of captureSnapshots is O(P×S) not O(P×T) where S=status count (5), T=task count

findings.title: TaskDependency table has no index on dependsOnTaskId — BFS cycle check and DELETE are sequential scans
findings.code_evidence:
model TaskDependency {
  id              String   @id @default(uuid())
  taskId          String
  dependsOnTaskId String
  createdAt       DateTime @default(now())
  ...
  @@unique([taskId, dependsOnTaskId])
  @@map("task_dependencies")
}
findings.description: TaskDependency has a composite unique on (taskId, dependsOnTaskId) which creates an index usable for lookups by taskId. However, there is no index on dependsOnTaskId alone. The reverse lookup `WHERE dependsOnTaskId = X` (used by the `dependents` relation in remove() to check if any task depends on the one being deleted) and the cycle-check BFS `WHERE taskId = currentTaskId` both scan the table. The dependents check on Task.remove fires `include: { dependents: true }` which generates WHERE task_id = X — covered. But the BFS check WHERE taskId = currentTaskId is also covered. The reverse WHERE depends_on_task_id = X for the `dependents` relation on Task (used in findOne) is NOT covered by the leading edge of the unique index.
findings.root_cause: The unique index is composite (taskId, dependsOnTaskId). Postgres can use it for taskId lookups but NOT for dependsOnTaskId-only lookups. The DependentTasks relation query uses dependsOnTaskId as the FK which scans the table.
findings.impact: Lookups on task.dependents (used in findOne, remove) require a sequential scan of task_dependencies when the table grows large. For a project with 1000 dependency edges, each findOne triggers a sequential scan.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `@@index([dependsOnTaskId])` to TaskDependency in schema.prisma and run a migration.
acceptance_criteria: schema.prisma has @@index([dependsOnTaskId]) on TaskDependency
EXPLAIN ANALYZE for dependents lookup shows Index Scan not Seq Scan

findings.title: TaskRACI has no index on userId — user-scoped RACI lookups scan the full table
findings.code_evidence:
model TaskRACI {
  id        String   @id @default(uuid())
  taskId    String
  userId    String
  role      RACIRole
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())
  ...
  @@unique([taskId, userId, role])
  @@map("task_raci")
}
findings.description: TaskRACI has a unique on (taskId, userId, role). The unique index can satisfy lookups by taskId but NOT by userId alone. Any query filtering by userId (e.g. 'what tasks is user X responsible for?') would require a sequential scan. Additionally the unique index leading on taskId means the userId position in the composite is not independently indexed.
findings.root_cause: No secondary index on userId for the TaskRACI model.
findings.impact: Future user-scoped RACI queries (if added) will scan the full table. Currently the raci relation in findOne filters by taskId (covered), so the immediate impact is limited. However it is a correctness gap for future queries.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `@@index([userId])` to TaskRACI in schema.prisma.
acceptance_criteria: schema.prisma has @@index([userId]) on TaskRACI
Query plan for userId-scoped RACI lookup shows Index Scan
findings.notes: Severity is important rather than blocking because current query paths filter by taskId (covered). This becomes blocking if user-scoped RACI views are added.

findings.title: comments.findAll default limit is 1000 — same ceiling as a de facto unbounded list
findings.description: Comments findAll uses the same default=1000 / ceiling=1000 pattern as projects. A task with thousands of comments (unlikely but possible for a long-lived task) would return all comments in one request. The include of author and task adds additional joins per comment row.
findings.root_cause: Default limit set to 1000 matching the ceiling, effectively paginating in a single page.
findings.impact: Low probability issue given comment volumes, but the pattern is inconsistent with good practice. Comment lists are typically rendered in-page and don't need more than 20-50 per view.
findings.suggested_fix: Lower default to 20, ceiling to 200.
acceptance_criteria: comments.findAll default limit is 20 or less
ceiling does not exceed 200

findings.title: All app pages are 'use client' — no RSC, no TanStack Query, no incremental hydration
findings.description: Every page in the App Router (dashboard, projects, tasks, leaves, and so on) is marked 'use client' and uses useEffect + useState for data fetching. This means the full JS bundle is shipped to the client on every navigation, no static or server-side rendering is used, and the page is blank until the client-side useEffect fires and the API response returns. TanStack Query is installed (in package.json via QueryProvider) but is not used in any page component for data fetching — all pages use useEffect + useState + direct service calls.
findings.root_cause: The frontend was built in a client-first pattern without adopting Server Components or TanStack Query's cache. The App Router's streaming/RSC capability is unused.
findings.impact: Every page load ships the full React component tree + all hooks to the client. There is no server-side data fetching, no stale-while-revalidate caching, and no background refetching. Users on slow connections see blank pages until multiple sequential API calls complete in useEffect.
findings.suggested_fix: Migrate data-fetching pages to use TanStack Query's useQuery hooks (already installed) for automatic caching, background refetch, and loading states. For static content (user list, departments), use RSC with server-side Prisma calls. At minimum, wrap the main data fetches in useQuery instead of useEffect+setState.
acceptance_criteria: At least one high-traffic page (dashboard or projects) uses useQuery for data fetching
No page uses useEffect+setState for primary data fetching when TanStack Query is available
findings.verification_command: grep -rn 'useQuery\|useMutation' apps/web/src/ apps/web/app/ | grep -v node_modules | wc -l
findings.notes: QueryProvider is correctly set up (apps/web/src/components/QueryProvider.tsx) but appears unused by actual page components.

findings.title: fetchManagersAndDepartments called on every Create button click — not cached between invocations
findings.description: Every click of the 'Create Project' button re-fetches the full user list and all departments, even if the modal was opened and closed moments before. These are reference data that change infrequently. The refetch costs 2 sequential network round-trips per modal open.
findings.root_cause: No caching of reference data; fetched on demand per modal open.
findings.impact: Minor UX degradation (slight delay before manager dropdown is populated on modal open). Not a critical performance issue but a common anti-pattern.
findings.suggested_fix: Fetch managers and departments once on page mount and cache in state, or use TanStack Query with a long staleTime (5 minutes) for reference data.
acceptance_criteria: Opening the Create Project modal does not fire new API requests if data was fetched in the last 5 minutes

findings.title: AuditLog has no index on createdAt+id — ORDER BY createdAt DESC, id DESC scans the full table
findings.description: The prevHash lookup inside every audit write sorts by (createdAt DESC, id DESC) LIMIT 1. Without a composite index on (createdAt, id), Postgres must sort the full audit_logs table to find the last row. As the audit table grows (the hash chain is immutable so rows are never deleted), this sort cost increases with table size.
findings.root_cause: No composite index on (createdAt DESC, id DESC) on audit_logs.
findings.impact: Every audit write fires a full table sort on audit_logs to find the prevHash. With 1 million audit rows this is a multi-second operation. The advisory lock holds during this sort, making the bottleneck compound (PERF-014 + PERF-025).
findings.suggested_fix: Add `@@index([createdAt(sort: Desc), id(sort: Desc)])` to the AuditLog model in schema.prisma. Verify with EXPLAIN ANALYZE.
acceptance_criteria: schema.prisma has a composite index on AuditLog(createdAt DESC, id DESC)
EXPLAIN ANALYZE for the prevHash query shows Index Scan not Sort+Seq Scan
findings.notes: Prisma may not support sort-direction indexes in schema.prisma PSL — a raw SQL migration may be required.

verified_clean: projects.findAll (L284-354): uses Promise.all for concurrent [findMany, count] — no sequential await
analytics.getProjectDetails (L386-473): uses Promise.all for two parallel timeEntry.groupBy calls instead of sequential queries
analytics.getActiveUsers (L238-258): single findMany with scoped OR filter — no fan-out
tasks.findAll (L356-414): uses Promise.all for concurrent [findMany, count]
time_tracking.getUserReport (L719-741): all aggregations pushed to Postgres via groupBy — no JS reduce over large arrays
time_tracking.getProjectReport (L807-985): 6 aggregation queries run in Promise.all — no sequential awaits
users.getUsersPresence (L1679-1810): consolidated to a single $queryRaw replacing 5 sequential findMany calls (PER-016)
projects.getSnapshots: uses @@index([date]) on ProjectSnapshot for date-range filtering
leaves.Leave model: has composite indexes on (userId, status), (validatorId, status), (startDate, endDate), (userId, startDate) covering all major query patterns
tasks.findForPlanningOverview: has a hard cap of PLANNING_HARD_CAP=500 rows
rbac.PermissionsService: has singleflight (inflight Map) to prevent Redis cache stampede on concurrent DB lookups for the same roleCode
projects.findAll (L357-379): uses task.groupBy for per-project progress instead of N tasks findMany
users.importUsers: pre-fetches departments, services, roles in parallel before the loop (validateImport does the same for existingUsers)
tasks.reorderSubtasks (L2050-2060): uses $transaction([...map]) batch instead of sequential updates
projects.getProjectsByUser (L1108-1135): includes tasks: { select: { status: true } } and computes progress in JS — acceptable since tasks take cap is inherited from membership filter
schema.prisma Project model: has indexes on createdById, managerId, sponsorId, archivedById, archivedAt covering FK and filter columns
schema.prisma Task model: has indexes on projectId, assigneeId, status, endDate, startDate, milestoneId, epicId, and composite (projectId, status)
schema.prisma TimeEntry model: has composite indexes on (userId, date), (thirdPartyId, date), (projectId, date), (taskId, userId)
schema.prisma Leave model: has 7 indexes covering all hot query dimensions
analytics.CacheService: user-scoped cache key prevents cross-user cache pollution
